nativeRange.habitat: Soft-bottom shallow reef environments — sand flats, muddy bottoms, seagrass beds, and back-reef lagoons. Anchored by a bulbous holdfast embedded in sediment rather than attached to hard substrate.

decorPreferences: open sand flat with deep substrate for holdfast anchoring
gentle to moderate flow — direct strong flow tears the soft thallus
isolation from delicate SPS corals (allelopathy risk)

goodWith: tangs and rabbitfish that generally ignore it
avoidWith: small mixed reefs where escape into sandbed is undesirable
tanks with Diadema-type urchins that may uproot the holdfast

imageCandidates.notes: Thomas Irvine, 1073x1188. In-situ underwater shot of the dark-green fan-shaped thallus anchored in the sandy/muddy bottom, showing the soft felt-like texture and sediment holdfast — the diagnostic look of A. nigricans in habitat.
Thomas Irvine, 1536x2048. Sharp whole fresh specimen on a light tray showing the complete fan blade and bulbous holdfast; out-of-water so lower ranked, but clearest view of overall morphology.
Thomas Irvine, 996x1770. Whole fresh specimen showing an elongated fan with stalk and holdfast; out-of-water reference shot, second morphology angle.

summary: The Mud Fan is a soft, leathery, fan-shaped green macroalga that anchors in sand or mud via a bulbous holdfast. It has been reported as invasive in some wild reef ecosystems and can become a tenacious nuisance in mixed reef tanks. It is best treated as a specialty display alga for keepers who are prepared to contain it.
careNotes:
The Mud Fan (Avrainvillea nigricans) is a soft, non-calcareous green macroalga — meaning its thallus (the fan-shaped body) is felt-like and rubbery rather than stiffened with calcium carbonate the way Halimeda or Penicillus are. Despite trade names like 'Leather Mud Fan', the texture is closer to a firm sponge than to anything calcified.

In the wild, the Mud Fan grows on soft sediment — sand flats, muddy back-reef bottoms, and seagrass beds — anchored by a bulbous, mat-like holdfast that penetrates several centimetres into the substrate. In the aquarium, a sand bed of at least 5 cm depth is the most natural placement. The species can also wedge its holdfast into rock crevices, but aquarists have reported that it becomes extremely difficult to remove from rock once established. The holdfast is tenacious and has been reported to regenerate from remnants left behind, which is a key reason this alga can be very hard to eradicate from a tank.

This species is genuinely uncommon in the mainstream aquarium trade and is not recommended for most reef aquariums. The genus produces bioactive secondary metabolites — notably dolabellane-type compounds and avrainvilleol — that have been documented in the marine chemistry literature and may drive mild allelopathic effects (chemical competition) on sensitive corals kept nearby. Handling established colonies with bare hands is generally not advised; gloves are sensible when uprooting the holdfast.

For aquarists who choose to keep it, generally advised parameters are: temperature around 22–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, nitrate 5–20 ppm, phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm. Lighting should be moderate and full-spectrum; strong actinic-only lighting is not sufficient. Flow should be gentle to moderate — direct strong flow has been reported to tear the soft thallus. Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and trace elements is generally recommended for macroalgae tanks. Propagation occurs through holdfast division or thallus fragmentation.

primary.name: Reeflex — Avrainvillea nigricans species ID
primary.notes: Most detailed aquarium-oriented care sheet found. Provides taxonomy, distribution, size (up to 15 cm), and explicit aquarium-suitability assessment ('not suitable for aquarium keeping' with a note on bioactive toxicity). Used as primary because the playbook-designated AlgaeBarn and Mosaic Macros searches both returned no Avrainvillea product page at time of research.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source providing canonical Mosaic parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm), the 4-method attachment standard, and the genus-strict sand-bed-tolerant list (Caulerpa + calcareous Halimedaceae/Udoteaceae genera only — explicitly excludes Avrainvillea).

additional.name: WoRMS — Avrainvillea nigricans (Decaisne, 1842)
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy verification: Plantae > Chlorophyta > Ulvophyceae > Bryopsidales > Dichotomosiphonaceae > Avrainvillea > A. nigricans. Status: accepted. Used in lieu of AlgaeBase, which returned CAPTCHA blocks at time of access.

additional.name: Marine Life Photography — Soft Fan Alga (Avrainvillea nigricans)
additional.notes: Field guide cross-check: confirms soft/leathery (non-calcareous) thallus, fan morphology, sand/seagrass habitat, ~20 cm size, and 1–10 m typical depth in the Florida Keys.

additional.name: Reef Central — Avrainvillea nigricans control thread
additional.notes: Aquarist field experience cross-check: confirms tenacious holdfast (penetrates rock crevices, regrows from remnants), limited grazing pressure from standard CUC, and possible biological control by certain sea slugs. Documents that some hobbyists do request and keep this species despite the pest reputation, supporting its inclusion as a specialty display algae rather than outright rejecting the entry.
